question: Among these labeled cases, what per-case signals distinguish the FAIL cases (false 'Yes' on an absent object) from the PASS cases? Propose composite signals as deterministic recipes over the available numeric columns.
observations: 121 labeled cases: 25 fail, 96 pass (base fail rate 0.207).
probe1_false_detection==1 vs fail: TP=25 FP=0 FN=0 TN=96. This probe appears to be a near-direct indicator of the fail condition.
Only 20/121 rows carry relative_attention_* features; any attention-based signal is descriptive on a small subset.
recommended_confirmatory_tests: Confirm probe1_flag predicts fail on a held-out split (two_group already attached).
Check whether generated_probe1_false_detection leaks the label definition.
On the attention subset, test focus_share / max-weight separation between pass and fail.
caveats: focus_share plot skipped: No module named 'matplotlib'
Exploratory only; no causal or statistical confirmation claimed.
relative_attention_* present on only 20/121 rows — attention signals are low-coverage and may not generalize.
probe1_false_detection may be (near-)definitional of the fail label; verify it is an independent predictor and not a leaked outcome.
marginal per-case signal verdicts are CI-based at alpha and NOT e-BH FDR-corrected across the signal family (unpaired test has no e-value)
conclusion: Standalone statistical analysis found a supported signal. Statistically supported: signal 'generated_probe1_false_detection' vs FAIL: [clustered bootstrap (unpaired)] effect=+0.8665 (B>A) CI=+0.6900..+1.0000 -> REJECT H0
